question: Azariah casts Kolaghan's Command targeting Azariah's Stonework Puma with the last 2 modes. In response, Nico sacrifices their Welding Jar to activate its ability, targeting the Stonework Puma. Does the Stonework Puma die?
expected_answer: Yes. The Kolaghan's Command's instructions are carried out in the order that they are written. When it attempts to destroy the Stonework Puma, it is regenerated. Then it is dealt 2 damage, and dies when state-based actions are checked. The regeneration shield no longer exists at this time.
model_answer:
Short answer: Yes — Stonework Puma ends up dead.

Explanation:
- Nico activates Welding Jar’s ability in response, sacrificing the Jar as the activation cost; the ability is an activated ability that targeted the Puma as it was activated (see CR 602.2b and CR 115.1c).
- When that ability resolves it “Regenerate[s] target artifact,” which creates a regeneration replacement effect that will protect that object the next time it would be destroyed this turn (see CR 701.19a; see also CR 614.8/CR 701.19 for what “regenerate” does).
- When Kolaghan’s Command later resolves you follow the chosen modes in the order printed on the card. The first chosen mode attempts to destroy the artifact (the Puma). The regeneration shield replaces that destruction — instead remove all damage marked on it and its controller taps it (and remove it from combat if it’s attacking or blocking) — and that regeneration shield is then used up (CR 701.19a; CR 614.8).
- The Command’s second chosen mode then deals 2 damage to the Puma. As a 2/2 creature, receiving 2 damage is lethal and will cause it to be destroyed by state-based actions; because the regeneration shield already protected it from the earlier destruction and was used up, it no longer prevents this destruction, so the Puma is put into its owner’s graveyard.

(If, instead, the Puma had left the battlefield or otherwise become an illegal target before the Command resolved, the Command would either not resolve or would have no effect on the Puma per the card’s rulings.)

question: Azariah casts Kolaghan's Command targeting Azariah's Stonework Puma with the last 2 modes. In response, Nico sacrifices their Welding Jar to activate its ability, targeting the Stonework Puma. Does the Stonework Puma die?
expected_answer: Yes. The Kolaghan's Command's instructions are carried out in the order that they are written. When it attempts to destroy the Stonework Puma, it is regenerated. Then it is dealt 2 damage, and dies when state-based actions are checked. The regeneration shield no longer exists at this time.
model_answer:
Yes — Stonework Puma dies.

Welding Jar’s ability resolves first and creates a regeneration shield for Stonework Puma. That shield protects it the next time it would be destroyed this turn: instead, damage is removed from it, it becomes tapped, and it’s removed from combat if applicable.

Kolaghan’s Command then resolves its chosen modes in printed order. First, “Destroy target artifact” tries to destroy Stonework Puma, and the regeneration shield replaces that destruction. That “next time” shield is used up.

Then Kolaghan’s Command deals 2 damage to Stonework Puma. After the Command finishes resolving, state-based actions see a 2/2 creature with 2 damage marked on it, so it has lethal damage and is destroyed. Regeneration could replace that destruction, but the Welding Jar shield was already consumed by the earlier “destroy target artifact” effect.

So the Welding Jar saves the Puma from the destroy-artifact mode, but not from the later lethal damage. Stonework Puma dies.

question: Azariah casts Kolaghan's Command targeting Azariah's Stonework Puma with the last 2 modes. In response, Nico sacrifices their Welding Jar to activate its ability, targeting the Stonework Puma. Does the Stonework Puma die?
expected_answer: Yes. The Kolaghan's Command's instructions are carried out in the order that they are written. When it attempts to destroy the Stonework Puma, it is regenerated. Then it is dealt 2 damage, and dies when state-based actions are checked. The regeneration shield no longer exists at this time.
model_answer:
When Azariah casts Kolaghan's Command choosing "Destroy target artifact" and "Kolaghan's Command deals 2 damage to any target," both modes target Stonework Puma, a 2/2 Artifact Creature, which is a legal target for each.

In response, Nico sacrifices Welding Jar to activate "Sacrifice this artifact: Regenerate target artifact," targeting the Stonework Puma. The sacrifice is paid as a cost when the ability is activated (CR 602.2b, applying CR 601.2h and 702.48b). This activated ability goes on the stack as an object (CR 113.1c) and resolves before Kolaghan's Command.

When the regeneration ability resolves, it sets up a regeneration shield on the Stonework Puma. Per CR 701.19a, this means: "The next time [Stonework Puma] would be destroyed this turn, instead remove all damage marked on it and its controller taps it." This is a one-time replacement effect (CR 701.8c) — a single shield that protects against the next destruction event only.

Both of Kolaghan's Command's targets are still legal, so the spell resolves. Per the spell's own ruling and CR 608.2c, the controller follows the chosen modes' instructions in the order they're printed:

1. **Destroy target artifact:** This would destroy the Stonework Puma. The regeneration shield replaces this destruction event (CR 701.8c, 701.19a) — the Puma is not destroyed; instead any damage on it is removed and it becomes tapped, and the shield is consumed.

2. **Kolaghan's Command deals 2 damage to any target:** Now this deals 2 damage to the Puma. The regeneration shield has already been used up by the destroy mode, so nothing protects it now. The 2/2 Puma now has 2 damage marked on it.

After Kolaghan's Command finishes resolving, state-based actions are checked (CR 704.2). Because the Stonework Puma has lethal damage marked (2 damage on 2 toughness), it is destroyed as a state-based action (CR 704.5g, CR 302.7). With no regeneration shield remaining, that destruction is not replaced.

**Yes, the Stonework Puma dies.** The key is the ordering: the "destroy" mode resolves first and exhausts the single regeneration shield from Welding Jar, leaving the subsequent 2 damage to deal lethal damage to the 2/2 with no shield left to save it.

question: Azariah casts Kolaghan's Command targeting Azariah's Stonework Puma with the last 2 modes. In response, Nico sacrifices their Welding Jar to activate its ability, targeting the Stonework Puma. Does the Stonework Puma die?
expected_answer: Yes. The Kolaghan's Command's instructions are carried out in the order that they are written. When it attempts to destroy the Stonework Puma, it is regenerated. Then it is dealt 2 damage, and dies when state-based actions are checked. The regeneration shield no longer exists at this time.
model_answer:
Looking at this interaction step by step:

## The Setup

Azariah casts **Kolaghan's Command** choosing the last two modes:
- Mode 3: "Destroy target artifact" (targeting Stonework Puma)
- Mode 4: "Kolaghan's Command deals 2 damage to any target" (targeting Stonework Puma)

In response, Nico sacrifices **Welding Jar** to activate its ability: "Regenerate target artifact" (targeting Stonework Puma).

## Resolution Order

By **CR 608.2**, when resolving spells and abilities, the last thing put on the stack resolves first.

**First, Welding Jar's ability resolves:**
By **CR 701.19a**, the regeneration ability creates a replacement effect that protects the Stonework Puma: "The next time [permanent] would be destroyed this turn, instead remove all damage marked on it and its controller taps it. If it's an attacking or blocking creature, remove it from combat."

**Second, Kolaghan's Command resolves:**
By the official rulings: "As the spell resolves, follow the instructions of the modes you chose in the order they are printed on the card."

So the modes resolve in printed order:
1. Mode 3 executes: "Destroy target artifact" - This attempts to destroy Stonework Puma
2. Mode 4 executes: "Kolaghan's Command deals 2 damage to any target" - This deals 2 damage to Stonework Puma

## The Key Interaction

When Mode 3 attempts to destroy the Stonework Puma, the regeneration shield applies. By **CR 614.8**, regeneration is a destruction-replacement effect that replaces the destruction with tapping the permanent and removing all damage from it.

Then Mode 4 deals 2 damage to the Puma. The Puma is a 2/2 creature. By **CR 704.5g**, a creature with damage marked on it equal to or greater than its toughness is destroyed. The 2 damage equals its toughness of 2.

**Crucially, the regeneration shield was already used up by Mode 3.** By **CR 701.19a**, the regeneration effect protects the permanent "the next time" it would be destroyed this turn—meaning it applies once. When state-based actions check after Mode 4 resolves and see the Puma has lethal damage, it will be destroyed, and there is no regeneration shield remaining to save it.

## Answer

**Yes, the Stonework Puma dies.** The regeneration shield from Welding Jar is consumed preventing the destruction from Mode 3 of Kolaghan's Command. The 2 damage from Mode 4 then causes the Puma to be destroyed by state-based actions (by **CR 704.5g**), with no regeneration shield remaining to prevent it.

question: Avery has a Wrenn and Six emblem and a Daze in their graveyard. Avery would like to return one of the Islands they controls to their hand then discard it to cast Daze. Can they do this?
expected_answer: Yes. Retrace is an additional cost. Returning a land to their hand is an alternative cost. Avery can decide to both pay an alternative cost and an additional cost. When deciding to cast the spell Avery will announce they decision to pay any alternative or additional costs, then afterwards they will pay those costs in any order.
model_answer:
Yes. The Wrenn and Six emblem gives instant and sorcery cards in your graveyard retrace (you may cast them from your graveyard by discarding a land as an additional cost), so Daze can be cast from the graveyard with that additional cost (CR 702.81a).

Casting a spell starts by putting the card on the stack; any continuous effects that modify the spell begin to apply as it is put on the stack (CR 601.2a, 611.2f). When you determine the total cost to cast the spell, you use the alternative cost you choose (return an Island) plus any additional costs (the retrace discard) (CR 601.2f; see CR 702.81a for retrace). The player pays all costs that don’t involve random elements or moving cards from the library first, in any order, then the remaining costs in any order (CR 601.2h).

Because both returning an Island from the battlefield to your hand (the alternative cost) and discarding a land from your hand (the retrace additional cost) are costs that can be ordered under CR 601.2h, you may choose to return an Island you control to your hand first and then discard that same Island to pay the retrace cost. Thus Avery can return an Island they control to their hand and then discard it to cast Daze from the graveyard.

question: Avery has a Wrenn and Six emblem and a Daze in their graveyard. Avery would like to return one of the Islands they controls to their hand then discard it to cast Daze. Can they do this?
expected_answer: Yes. Retrace is an additional cost. Returning a land to their hand is an alternative cost. Avery can decide to both pay an alternative cost and an additional cost. When deciding to cast the spell Avery will announce they decision to pay any alternative or additional costs, then afterwards they will pay those costs in any order.
model_answer:
Yes. Avery can do this, assuming they otherwise can legally cast Daze.

Avery’s Wrenn and Six emblem is controlled by Avery by CR 114.2, and its Oracle text gives “Instant and sorcery cards in your graveyard” retrace. Since Daze is an instant card in Avery’s graveyard, retrace allows Avery to cast it from the graveyard by discarding a land card in addition to paying its other costs. Avery can begin casting it because an effect allows that, as required by CR 601.3.

When casting Daze, Avery moves it from the graveyard to the stack as part of casting it, by CR 601.2 and CR 601.2a. For Daze’s cost, Avery may choose Daze’s alternative cost: “return an Island you control to its owner’s hand rather than pay this spell’s mana cost.” Daze’s official ruling says that to determine total cost, you start with the mana cost or alternative cost you’re paying, such as Daze’s alternative cost, then apply increases and reductions. Retrace’s discard-a-land requirement is paid “in addition to” Daze’s other costs, per the Wrenn and Six emblem text.

The key point is cost payment order: when paying the total cost, Avery pays all applicable costs in any order, by CR 601.2h. So Avery may pay Daze’s alternative cost first by returning an Island they control to their hand, then pay the retrace additional cost by discarding that land card.

So the answer is: yes, Avery can return an Island they control to their hand for Daze’s alternative cost, then discard that same Island to pay retrace.

question: Avery has a Wrenn and Six emblem and a Daze in their graveyard. Avery would like to return one of the Islands they controls to their hand then discard it to cast Daze. Can they do this?
expected_answer: Yes. Retrace is an additional cost. Returning a land to their hand is an alternative cost. Avery can decide to both pay an alternative cost and an additional cost. When deciding to cast the spell Avery will announce they decision to pay any alternative or additional costs, then afterwards they will pay those costs in any order.
model_answer:
Yes—but only by using a *different* land. Here's why.

The Wrenn and Six emblem gives instant and sorcery cards in your graveyard retrace: "You may cast this card from your graveyard by discarding a land card as an additional cost to cast it" (CR 702.81a). So to cast Daze from the graveyard, Avery must discard a land card in addition to paying Daze's other costs.

Daze itself has an alternative cost: "You may return an Island you control to its owner's hand rather than pay this spell's mana cost." When the total cost of the spell is determined, you start with the alternative cost you're paying (returning an Island), then apply any additional costs such as retrace's discard (CR 601.2f; CR 118.9d confirms that additional costs are applied on top of an alternative cost). So the proposed total cost is: *return an Island you control to its owner's hand* + *discard a land card*.

The decisive point is how costs are actually paid. Under CR 601.2h, after the total cost is determined the player pays it: first all costs that don't involve random elements or moving objects from the library to a public zone, in any order, then any remaining costs. The discard portion of the retrace cost requires Avery to discard a land card—which means the card must be in their hand at the moment they pay that cost. The Island returned by Daze's alternative cost isn't in the hand when costs begin to be paid; returning it is itself one of the costs being paid. Each cost is a separate action that must be satisfied with its own resources (CR 118.10: "Each payment of a cost applies to only one spell, ability, or effect"). A single Island can't be both the thing returned by the alternative cost and the land card discarded for the retrace cost—those are two distinct payments, and the discard payment has nothing in hand to act on if you try to use the very Island you're returning.

So **no**, Avery cannot return a single Island to hand and then discard that same Island to pay the retrace cost.

Avery **can**, however, cast Daze via retrace if they pay both costs from separate sources: return one Island to hand for Daze's alternative cost *and* discard a different land card that is already in their hand for the retrace cost. With those two separate cards, the casting is legal.

model_answer:
Looking at this question, I need to determine whether Avery can return an Island to their hand and then discard it to cast Daze using the retrace ability granted by Wrenn and Six's emblem.

Let me work through the casting process step by step:

**The Wrenn and Six Emblem**
According to the oracle text, the emblem grants: "Instant and sorcery cards in your graveyard have retrace." The retrace reminder text states: "You may cast instant and sorcery cards from your graveyard by discarding a land card in addition to paying their other costs."

**Casting Daze with Retrace**
When Avery wants to cast Daze from their graveyard using retrace, they must follow the steps in CR 601.2:

1. **Proposal (CR 601.2a)**: Avery moves Daze from the graveyard to the stack.

2. **Choose modes, targets, etc.** (CR 601.2b-d): Avery chooses the target spell to counter.

3. **Determine costs (CR 601.2f)**: Here's where the key interaction occurs. Daze has:
   - A mana cost of {1}{U}
   - An alternative cost: "You may return an Island you control to its owner's hand rather than pay this spell's mana cost"
   - The retrace ability adds: "discarding a land card in addition to paying their other costs"

   By CR 601.2f, Avery can choose to use Daze's alternative cost (returning an Island) instead of the mana cost. The retrace cost (discarding a land) is added to whichever cost is chosen.

4. **Pay costs (CR 601.2h)**: According to CR 601.2h, "The player pays the total cost. First, they pay all costs that don't involve random elements or moving objects from the library to a public zone, in any order."

**The Critical Point**
Both returning the Island (for Daze's alternative cost) and discarding a land card (for retrace) are costs that must be paid during step 601.2h. Importantly, CR 601.2h allows the player to pay these costs "in any order."

Therefore, Avery can:
1. First return an Island to their hand (paying part of the alternative cost)
2. Then discard that same Island from their hand (paying the retrace cost)

**Answer: Yes, Avery can do this.**

The key is that by CR 601.2h, costs are paid in any order chosen by the player. Avery can return the Island first (which puts it in their hand), then immediately discard it to satisfy the retrace requirement. Both actions occur during the same step of casting the spell (paying costs), so the Island is available in hand to be discarded after being returned.

question: Autumn controls Thran Lens and Giver of Runes which has given The Fourteenth Doctor protection from colorless. Autumn then casts Replenish with Mantle of Leadership in the graveyard. Can it enchant The Fourteenth Doctor? If so, what happens?
expected_answer:
Unclear. Auras entering the battlefield can only enchant legal permanents, but it's not specified whether the legality is checked considering their characteristics in the previous zone or on the battlefield.

If it can enchant The Fourteenth Doctor, it will die the next time state-based actions are checked.
model_answer:
Short answer: No. Mantle of Leadership cannot end up attached to The Fourteenth Doctor in this situation. (If there is no other legal creature for Mantle to enchant, it stays in the graveyard; if it is returned attached to some other legal creature, it will attach to that creature instead.)

Explanation and rules grounding, step by step:

1) Thran Lens makes permanents colorless, so the Mantle that would be put onto the battlefield by Replenish will be colorless as it enters. This follows from Thran Lens’s oracle text: “All permanents are colorless.” (Thran Lens oracle.)

2) The Fourteenth Doctor currently has protection from colorless granted by Giver of Runes. Protection is a static ability that prevents certain interactions; the Giver of Runes ruling explicitly states that “protection from colorless means that the target creature ... can't be enchanted or equipped by colorless Auras or Equipment.” (Giver of Runes oracle and ruling; see also the definition of protection as a static ability, CR 702.16a.)

3) What Replenish does and what it may place onto the battlefield: Replenish “Return all enchantment cards from your graveyard to the battlefield,” and its rulings say it determines what enchantments to return when its resolution starts and that “Auras can only be placed on permanents that were on the battlefield before this effect started to resolve.” It also says “Auras with nothing to enchant remain in your graveyard.” (Replenish oracle and rulings.)

4) Interaction outcome: Because of (1) and (2), Mantle would be a colorless Aura and The Fourteenth Doctor is a permanent with protection from colorless; therefore Mantle is not a legal enchantment for The Fourteenth Doctor (Giver ruling: “can't be enchanted ... by colorless Auras”). If Replenish’s resolution attempts to put Mantle onto The Fourteenth Doctor, that attachment is illegal; such an Aura attached to an illegal object is put into its owner’s graveyard as a state-based action (CR 704.5m) and protection’s rule about Auras applies (CR 702.16c / Giver ruling). Alternatively, if there is no legal permanent among those that were on the battlefield before Replenish began to resolve that Mantle can legally enchant, the Replenish ruling says Mantle remains in your graveyard.

5) Summary of practical results:
- Mantle of Leadership cannot legally remain attached to The Fourteenth Doctor. (Answer: No.) (Giver ruling; Thran Lens oracle; CR 702.16c; CR 704.5m.)
- If there is some other creature that was on the battlefield before Replenish started resolving that Mantle can legally enchant, Mantle may be returned attached to that creature instead (Replenish rulings). If there is no such legal creature, Mantle stays in the graveyard (Replenish ruling).
- If Mantle nevertheless is momentarily put onto The Fourteenth Doctor (for example, because of how attachments are placed), state-based actions will immediately move that illegal Aura to its owner’s graveyard (CR 704.5m; CR 702.16c).

If you want, I can walk through an explicit timeline showing Replenish’s resolution and the state-based-action check step by step.

question: Autumn controls Thran Lens and Giver of Runes which has given The Fourteenth Doctor protection from colorless. Autumn then casts Replenish with Mantle of Leadership in the graveyard. Can it enchant The Fourteenth Doctor? If so, what happens?

Unclear. Auras entering the battlefield can only enchant legal permanents, but it's not specified whether the legality is checked considering their characteristics in the previous zone or on the battlefield.

If it can enchant The Fourteenth Doctor, it will die the next time state-based actions are checked.
model_answer:
No. **Mantle of Leadership can’t enchant The Fourteenth Doctor** in this situation.

- **Giver of Runes** gave **The Fourteenth Doctor** protection from colorless. Protection is a static ability by **CR 702.16a**, and a permanent with protection “can’t be enchanted by Auras that have the stated quality” by **CR 702.16c**. The Giver of Runes ruling states the same for protection from colorless: the creature “can’t be enchanted … by colorless Auras.”
- Therefore, **The Fourteenth Doctor can’t be enchanted by Mantle of Leadership** while Thran Lens is making Mantle colorless.

When **Replenish** resolves, it returns enchantment cards from Autumn’s graveyard to the battlefield, but its Oracle text says “Auras with nothing to enchant remain in your graveyard.” Its ruling also says you must return Auras if possible. question: Autumn controls Thran Lens and Giver of Runes which has given The Fourteenth Doctor protection from colorless. Autumn then casts Replenish with Mantle of Leadership in the graveyard. Can it enchant The Fourteenth Doctor? If so, what happens?
expected_answer:
Unclear. Auras entering the battlefield can only enchant legal permanents, but it's not specified whether the legality is checked considering their characteristics in the previous zone or on the battlefield.

If it can enchant The Fourteenth Doctor, it will die the next time state-based actions are checked.
model_answer:
# Setting Up the Situation

Let me identify the relevant continuous effects:

1. **Thran Lens** says "All permanents are colorless." This makes every permanent—including Mantle of Leadership—colorless. Per its ruling, this doesn't make them artifacts; it simply removes their color.

2. **Giver of Runes** has given The Fourteenth Doctor **protection from colorless**.

3. **Replenish** is resolving and will "Return all enchantment cards from your graveyard to the battlefield," including **Mantle of Leadership**, an Aura with "Enchant creature."

# Does Protection from Colorless Apply?

Mantle of Leadership is an Aura. Under normal circumstances it's white. But Thran Lens makes **all permanents colorless**. The key question is whether Mantle of Leadership is colorless at the point it's being attached.

By CR 702.16c, "A permanent or player with protection can't be enchanted by Auras that have the stated quality." The Giver of Runes ruling confirms this directly for our case: "protection from colorless means that the target creature... can't be enchanted or equipped by colorless Auras or Equipment."

Now, Mantle of Leadership is in the graveyard while Replenish resolves. A card in the graveyard is not a permanent, so Thran Lens (which affects "all permanents") does **not** make the graveyard card colorless—there it retains its printed white color. But Thran Lens is a continuous effect that applies to permanents on the battlefield. As the Aura is put onto the battlefield and would attach, it becomes a permanent and Thran Lens applies to it, making it colorless.

# Replenish's Forced Attachment

By Replenish's ruling, "You must return Auras if possible, even if this means enchanting an opponent's permanent." Replenish puts the Aura onto the battlefield attached to a legal creature. The Aura must enchant a creature that was on the battlefield before Replenish began resolving (per its ruling). The Fourteenth Doctor qualifies as such a creature.

When the Aura enters attached to The Fourteenth Doctor, Thran Lens immediately makes it colorless. At that point, The Fourteenth Doctor has protection from colorless, so the colorless Aura is now attached illegally.

# State-Based Action

By CR 702.16c, an Aura with the protected-against quality attached to a creature with that protection "will be put into their owners' graveyards as a state-based action." More generally, CR 704.5m states that an Aura attached to an illegal object "is put into its owner's graveyard."

So once Replenish finishes resolving, state-based actions are checked. Mantle of Leadership, now colorless (via Thran Lens) and attached to a creature with protection from colorless, is an illegal attachment. It is immediately put into the graveyard as a state-based action.

# Could It Have Been Attached Elsewhere?

Replenish requires you to return the Aura "if possible." If there is another legal creature to enchant (one without protection from colorless), Autumn would be required to attach Mantle of Leadership there instead, since you must return Auras if possible to a legal target. But if The Fourteenth Doctor is the only available creature, the Aura still enters the battlefield attached to it (Replenish doesn't check protection at the moment it places the Aura the way targeting would, since Replenish doesn't target).

# Conclusion

**No, it cannot legally stay enchanting The Fourteenth Doctor.** Because Thran Lens makes Mantle of Leadership colorless once it's on the battlefield, and The Fourteenth Doctor has protection from colorless, the Aura is an illegal attachment. Per CR 702.16c and CR 704.5m, it is immediately put into Autumn's graveyard as a state-based action after Replenish resolves. The Aura ends up back in the graveyard.

(One caveat: this relies on Mantle of Leadership being colorless at the time of attachment. Since Thran Lens only affects permanents on the battlefield, the Aura is treated as colorless precisely once it enters the battlefield as a permanent—which is exactly when the protection check via state-based actions matters. So the result is that it goes to the graveyard.)

model_answer:
Looking at this interaction step by step:

## Initial State Analysis

By CR 205.1 and the Thran Lens ruling, all permanents on the battlefield are colorless. This includes The Fourteenth Doctor, which is normally red, green, white, and blue.

Giver of Runes has given The Fourteenth Doctor protection from colorless. By CR 702.16a and the Giver of Runes ruling, this means The Fourteenth Doctor "can't be enchanted or equipped by colorless Auras or Equipment."

## When Replenish Resolves

When Replenish resolves, it attempts to return Mantle of Leadership from the graveyard to the battlefield.

**Key Question: What color is Mantle of Leadership as it enters the battlefield?**

According to the Thran Lens ruling: "Does not prevent a spell or ability from adding color to permanents after this effect is applied." This means Thran Lens's effect applies first in layer 5 (by CR 613.1d for color-changing effects), making all permanents colorless.

However, Mantle of Leadership has a white mana cost {1}{W}. By CR 202.2, an object's color is determined by the color of the mana symbols in its mana cost. As Mantle of Leadership enters the battlefield, it is white.

**But then Thran Lens immediately applies:** The second Thran Lens ruling states "Does not make the permanents into artifacts. They are simply without color." Once Mantle of Leadership is on the battlefield, Thran Lens's continuous effect makes it colorless.

## The Critical Timing Issue

By CR 303.4f (for Auras entering via a spell or ability other than being cast), the Aura enters the battlefield attached to the object or player it can legally enchant. This happens as a single event.

The question is whether protection checks the Aura's color:
1. As it enters (when it's still white), or
2. After it's on the battlefield (when Thran Lens makes it colorless)

By CR 702.16c: "A permanent or player with protection can't be enchanted by Auras that have the stated quality." The protection ability checks whether the Aura has the stated quality (colorless) at the time it would become attached.

**As Mantle of Leadership enters the battlefield, it is white** (from its mana cost). At that moment, The Fourteenth Doctor has protection from colorless, not protection from white. Therefore, the protection does not prevent the attachment.

## After Attachment

Once Mantle of Leadership is on the battlefield attached to The Fourteenth Doctor, Thran Lens's continuous effect makes it colorless. Now we have a colorless Aura attached to a creature with protection from colorless.

By CR 702.16c: "Such Auras attached to the permanent or player with protection will be put into their owners' graveyards as a state-based action." By CR 704.5m, this happens because the Aura is attached to an illegal object.

## Answer

**Yes, Mantle of Leadership can initially enchant The Fourteenth Doctor** because it is white as it enters the battlefield, and The Fourteenth Doctor only has protection from colorless at that moment.

question: Nico controls Kinsbaile Borderguard with 3 +1/+1 counters it. During their main phase, Ainsley casts Black Sun's Zenith to put 4 -1/-1 counters on Kinsbaile Borderguard. How many tokens will Nico create when Kinsbaile Borderguard's trigger resolves?
expected_answer:
Seven.

When state-based actions are checked, 3 of each counter are removed from Kinsbaile Borderguard and Kinsbaile Borderguard will die.Then, the trigger from Kinsbaile Borderguard will be put on the stack. When that trigger resolves, it must use the last known information of Kinsbaile Borderguard to determine the number of counters on it, and when Kinsbaile Borderguard was last on the battlefield immediately before it died it had 7 counters on it.

1) Black Sun's Zenith with X=4 puts four -1/-1 counters on Kinsbaile Borderguard when the spell resolves (per Black Sun's Zenith oracle text). That makes the creature have 3 +1/+1 counters and 4 -1/-1 counters immediately after the spell’s effect is applied. (Black Sun's Zenith oracle.)

2) When a permanent has both +1/+1 and -1/-1 counters, state-based actions remove N +1/+1 and N -1/-1 counters, where N is the smaller of the two amounts; state-based actions happen automatically. Apply that here: N = min(3,4) = 3, so three +1/+1 and three -1/-1 are removed as a state-based action, leaving 0 +1/+1 and 1 -1/-1 on the creature. (CR 122.3; CR 704.5q; CR 704.1.)

3) The creature’s having 0 toughness (1 base toughness minus the remaining one -1/-1) and the counter removals are state-based actions performed at the same time. When a state-based action causes a permanent to leave the battlefield at the same time other state-based actions are performed, that permanent’s last known information is taken from the game state before any of those state-based actions were performed. Therefore the “information about the creature when it left the battlefield” includes the counters it had immediately after Black Sun’s Zenith resolved (i.e., before the counter-pair removals). (CR 704.8.)

4) Kinsbaile Borderguard’s death trigger creates one 1/1 Kithkin token for each counter that was on it when it left the battlefield, and that counts counters of any kind, not just +1/+1 counters. (Kinsbaile Borderguard oracle and its ruling.)

5) Combining the above: immediately after the spell’s effect resolved (and thus as last known information for when it dies) the creature had 3 +1/+1 counters and 4 -1/-1 counters = 7 counters total. The trigger therefore creates 7 one‑one Kithkin Soldier tokens.

Conclusion: Nico creates 7 tokens.

The Ingester rulings state its power and toughness "are constantly updated if the exiled card's power and/or toughness change" — and if a card is no longer in exile, it no longer contributes its power and toughness to the calculation.

The static ability still refers to all cards exiled by the trigger and its copy, but only **Giant Octopus** remains in exile (3/3). Isamaru no longer contributes anything.

# Result

After Isamaru is moved to the graveyard, the bonus becomes:
- X = 3 (Giant Octopus only)
- Y = 3

Phyrexian Ingester = 3/3 base + 3/+3 = **6/6**.

So **Phyrexian Ingester will be 6/6** once Isamaru is removed from exile.

question: Arielle has seven cards in their hand, controls Trusted Advisor, and casts Recycle. After Arielle discards at the end of the turn, how many cards should they have in their hand?
expected_answer: Two. Trusted Advisor adds two to the maximum hand size (making it nine), and Recycle then sets the maximum hand to two.
model_answer:
Arielle will have 4 cards in hand (they discard 3).

Reasoning:

- Trusted Advisor’s effect is “Your maximum hand size is increased by two.” Recycle’s effect is “Your maximum hand size is two.”
- These are continuous effects that modify a player’s maximum hand size. Because Trusted Advisor was already on the battlefield when Arielle cast Recycle, Recycle has the later timestamp and first establishes the maximum as 2; Trusted Advisor’s earlier-effect then modifies that maximum by +2, resulting in a final maximum hand size of 4 (this is the same interaction illustrated by the official rulings for those cards).
- The active player discards down to their maximum hand size as the first action of the cleanup step (CR 514.1). To discard a card means to move it from hand to graveyard (CR 701.9a).
- Arielle begins cleanup with seven cards and a final maximum of four, so they discard three cards and end the cleanup step with four cards in hand.

question: Autumn controls Emeria Shepherd. They play Umbral Expanse, and target Wei Infantry in their graveyard. In response, Nia activates Alchemist's Refuge. When that ability resolves, they casts Blood Moon. When Emeria Shepherd's ability resolves, where is Wei Infantry?
expected_answer:
Autumn's hand.

1) A land (Umbral Expanse) entered the battlefield under Autumn’s control, so Emeria Shepherd’s landfall ability triggered. Emeria’s oracle text: “Whenever a land you control enters, you may return target nonland permanent card from your graveyard to your hand. If that land is a Plains, you may return that nonland permanent card to the battlefield instead.” And Emeria’s rulings confirm landfall triggers whenever a land enters (Emeria Shepherd oracle & rulings 4–5).

2) The triggered ability is on the stack, and before it resolved Nia caused Blood Moon to enter the battlefield (by using Alchemist’s Refuge to cast it in the available window). Alchemist’s Refuge’s oracle allows casting spells as though they had flash, enabling Blood Moon to be cast in response to the trigger (Alchemist’s Refuge oracle and ruling).

3) When Blood Moon resolved, it changed all nonbasic lands into Mountains and caused them to lose their other land types and abilities: “Nonbasic lands will lose any other land types and abilities they had. They will gain the land type Mountain and gain the ability ‘{T}: Add {R}.’” (Blood Moon oracle/rulings 3–4). CR 305.7 (in the retrieved rules) describes that setting a land’s subtype to a basic land type causes it to lose its old land types and abilities, which is what Blood Moon’s effect does.

4) The triggered landfall ability resolves later and must look at the current characteristics of the land that entered. By rule, a triggered ability that acts on an object in the zone the object moved to checks that object as it exists at resolution (see CR 603.6: “During resolution, these abilities look for the object in the zone that it moved to.”). At that time Umbral Expanse is a Mountain (a nonbasic land made a Mountain by Blood Moon) and no longer has the Plains land type it printed with (Umbral Expanse oracle shows its printed types are Plains Swamp, but Blood Moon caused it to lose those types and gain Mountain).

5) Because the land is not a Plains when Emeria’s ability resolves, the conditional part “If that land is a Plains, you may return that nonland permanent card to the battlefield instead” is false. Therefore the ability can only return the targeted card from the graveyard to Autumn’s hand (Emeria Shepherd oracle text).

Conclusion: When Emeria Shepherd’s ability resolves after Blood Moon is on the battlefield, Wei Infantry is returned to Autumn’s hand (not put onto the battlefield).

question: Addison controls Alhammarret's Archive, Thought Reflection, Blood Scrivener, Nefarious Lich, and Words of Worship. Noa controls Chains of Mephistopheles, Alms Collector and Rain of Gore. Neither player has any cards in hand. Addison activates Words of Worship once, then moves to their draw step. What is the greatest number of cards that Addison can end up with in their hand, assuming they make the best possible choices? What else happens in that scenario?
expected_answer:
At most Addison can end up with 29 cards in their hand. Rain of Gore only applies to life gained due to a spell or ability, not a turn-based action, so it won't apply to any life gain caused by Words of Worship. Whenever multiple replacement effects apply to an event (such as Addison drawing a card), the affected player will chose which one to apply first. Once a replacement effect has been applied to an event, it will not apply again to that event or any events contained inside that event.

* The event begins as "draw a card". * Addison chooses to apply Blood Scrivener's effect. The event is "Draw 2 cards, lose 1 life". * Addison must apply Alms Collector's effect. The event is "Draw a card, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Addison chooses to apply Words of Worship's effect to their draw. The event is "Gain 5 life, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Addison must apply Alhammarret's Archive's first effect to the life gain. The event is "Gain 10 life, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Addison must apply Nefarious Lich's effect to the life gain. The event is "Draw 10 cards, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Addison must apply Thought Reflection's effect to the first card draw. The event is "Draw 2 cards, draw 9 cards, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Addison draws a card. The event is "Draw a card, draw 9 cards, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Addison chooses to apply Chains of Mephistopheles's effect to the first card draw. The event is "Discard a card, draw a card, draw 9 cards, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Addison discards a card. The event is "Draw a card, draw 9 cards, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Addison must apply Alhammarret's Archive's effect to the first card draw. The event is "Draw 2 cards, draw 9 cards, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Addison draws 2 cards. The event is "Draw 9 cards, Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * For the next 9 cards draws, Addison chooses to apply and perform Chains of Mephistopheles's effect followed by Alhammarret's Archive and Thought Reflection's effects, making them all "discard a card, draw 4 cards". The event is "Noa draws a card, lose 1 life". * Noa must apply Chains of Mephistopheles's effect to the card draw, putting a card from their library into their graveyard. * Addison loses 1 life.

Addison has 29 cards in their hand and 10 cards in their graveyard. Noa has 1 card in their graveyard. Addison has lost 1 life.
model_answer:
Addison can end up with 7 cards in hand. In that scenario Noa draws 1 card, Addison loses 1 life, and the Words of Worship activation is used up.

Why: Words of Worship replaces Addison’s turn‑based draw with “gain 5 life.” Addison chooses to have Nefarious Lich replace that would‑gain‑life event with “draw 5 cards” instead, so the turn‑based draw has become an instruction to draw five cards. Noa’s Alms Collector then replaces that single “draw five” instruction (Alms replaces an instruction to draw two or more cards before effects that act on individual draws apply) with two separate single‑card draws: “Addison draws 1” and “Noa draws 1.” (Noa therefore draws 1.)

Addison’s single draw is the first card they draw as part of their draw‑step draw, so Alhammarret’s Archive’s exemption for “the first one you draw in each of your draw steps” applies to that first individual draw and does not double it. Replacement effects that change individual draws do apply to that draw; Addison, wanting to maximize cards, orders them to get the best outcome: apply Blood Scrivener first (its condition is met because Addison has no cards in hand), which replaces “draw 1” with “draw 2 and you lose 1 life” (Addison loses 1 life), and then apply Thought Reflection, which doubles that “draw 2” into “draw 4.” Those four draws are performed one at a time; Archive does not double the very first individual draw of the draw step (the first of those four), but it does replace each of the remaining three individual draws with two draws. So the individual draws resolve as 1 (first, not doubled by Archive) + 2 + 2 + 2 = 7 cards drawn by Addison.

Words of Worship’s stored replacement is consumed when this sequence occurs. Rain of Gore does not change the result because Addison (the affected player) chooses to have Nefarious Lich replace the would‑gain‑life event with drawing before any replacement that would make them lose life would be applied. Chains of Mephistopheles does not affect the result because the draw that occurs for Addison here is the first card of their draw step and is therefore exempt from Chains.

Addison’s draw from Alms Collector is handled first. Addison uses the already-activated **Words of Worship** on it, so instead of drawing, Addison would gain 5 life. Addison applies **Alhammarret’s Archive** to make that 10 life, then applies **Nefarious Lich** to replace that life gain with drawing 10 cards. Addison gains no life, so **Rain of Gore** does not make Addison lose life.

**Alms Collector** has already applied to this event, so it does not apply again to the resulting draw-ten event; CR 614.5 says a replacement effect doesn’t invoke itself repeatedly and gets only one opportunity to affect an event or any modified events that replace it.

The ten cards from Nefarious Lich are drawn one at a time. Since no card has actually been drawn yet in Addison’s draw step, the first actual card drawn is still the first card Addison draws in that draw step, so it is exempt from **Alhammarret’s Archive**’s draw replacement and **Chains of Mephistopheles**.

For the first of the ten Lich draws, Addison applies **Thought Reflection**, making it two draws. The first of those is the first actual draw of the draw step and is drawn normally. The second is not exempt from Chains, so Addison applies **Chains of Mephistopheles**, discards the card just drawn, then applies **Alhammarret’s Archive** to the resulting draw and draws two cards. Net result from the first Lich draw: **2 cards in hand**.

For each of the remaining nine Lich draws, Addison has cards in hand, so Addison applies **Chains of Mephistopheles** first: discard one card, then draw a card. That resulting draw is then replaced by both **Thought Reflection** and **Alhammarret’s Archive**, so Addison draws four cards. Each of those nine draws is net **+3 cards** in hand, for +27 total.

So Addison finishes this draw-step sequence with:

- **29 cards in hand**.
- **Addison lost 1 life** from Blood Scrivener.
- **Addison discarded 10 cards** total to Chains of Mephistopheles.
- **Noa mills 1 card** from Chains of Mephistopheles instead of drawing their Alms Collector card, since Noa has no cards in hand and it is not Noa’s draw step.
- Addison gained no life, so **Rain of Gore causes no life loss**.

If the turn later reaches cleanup with no further changes, Addison will normally discard down to maximum hand size then, but the maximum immediately after this draw-step sequence is **29**.

model_answer:
Let's work through this carefully, tracking the two events separately: the Words of Worship activation, and the draw step.

## Setup

Addison controls Alhammarret's Archive, Thought Reflection, Blood Scrivener, Nefarious Lich, and Words of Worship. Both players have empty hands.

## Step 1: The Words of Worship activation

Addison activates Words of Worship once. Its effect: "The next time you would draw a card this turn, you gain 5 life instead." This sets up a replacement effect waiting for Addison's next draw.

## Step 2: The draw step — the turn-based draw

Immediately after the draw step begins, the active player draws a card (CR 504.1, CR 703.4d). This single draw event has several replacement effects competing to apply, and Addison, as the affected player, chooses the order to apply them (CR 616.1, and the Archive/Thought Reflection rulings).

Two of the opponent's effects and one of Addison's are keyed to "the first one [you/they] draw in each of [your/their] draw steps":

- **Chains of Mephistopheles** is explicitly exempt for "the first one they draw in each of their draw steps," and its ruling confirms "A player's normal card draw on their turn is exempt." So this turn-based draw is not touched by Chains.
- **Alhammarret's Archive** likewise reads "except the first one you draw in each of your draw steps," so the Archive's doubling does not apply to this draw.

Importantly, the "first draw of the draw step" exemption is keyed to the literal turn-based draw of the draw step — this draw — regardless of how it ends up being replaced. Once Addison replaces this draw with other effects, the resulting events are no longer "the first one you draw in your draw step," so the Archive and Chains can apply to those resulting events normally.

That leaves these replacement effects competing for the turn-based draw:
- **Words of Worship**: draw → gain 5 life (queued from Step 1)
- **Thought Reflection**: draw → draw two cards
- **Blood Scrivener**: draw with no cards in hand → draw two cards and lose 1 life

The optimal play to maximize cards is to apply **Words of Worship first**: "draw a card" becomes "gain 5 life instead." This is now a life-gain event, and it is no longer a draw, so Thought Reflection and Blood Scrivener don't apply to it.

Now the life-gain replacement effects apply:
- **Alhammarret's Archive**: "If you would gain life, you gain twice that much life instead" → 5 becomes 10.
- **Nefarious Lich**: "If you would gain life, draw that many cards instead."

To maximize cards, apply the Archive first (gain 10 instead of 5), then **Nefarious Lich**: gaining 10 life becomes "draw 10 cards." Per CR 121.7, when a replacement effect results in card draws, those draws happen one at a time. The Lich won't act on this event again.

**Rain of Gore** never triggers: the Lich converted the life gain into draws, so no life is actually gained, and there's no net life loss either.

## Step 3: The resulting draws

Addison now performs 10 individual card draws, one at a time (CR 121.2, CR 121.7). Crucially, **none of these is the turn-based first draw of the draw step** — that draw was replaced. So for each of these 10 draws, all the "draw a card" replacements apply, including Alhammarret's Archive's doubling and Noa's Chains of Mephistopheles.

For the **first** of these 10 draws, Addison's hand is empty. The competing replacements are Thought Reflection, Blood Scrivener, Alhammarret's Archive (all "draw two or more"), and Noa's Alms Collector and Chains.

This is where the opponent's effects dominate:

- **Alms Collector**: "If an opponent would draw two or more cards, instead you and that player each draw a card." Its ruling is decisive — it "applies to an instruction to draw more than one card before any replacement effects apply to individual cards drawn." So as soon as Thought Reflection (or Blood Scrivener, or the Archive) would turn one of Addison's draws into "draw two or more," Alms Collector replaces that entire instruction with "Addison draws one and Noa draws one." Once applied to an event, it can't apply again to the resulting draws.

To maximize cards, Addison should apply Alms Collector's replacement, then on the resulting single card draw apply the doublers. By the Alms Collector ruling, once it has modified the event, Thought Reflection can double the resulting draw without Alms Collector applying again — but doubling produces "draw two," which is again a two-or-more instruction, except Alms Collector is already used up on this event. However, **Chains of Mephistopheles** now governs Addison's empty-hand single draws.

- **Chains of Mephistopheles**: When Addison would draw with an empty hand, Chains replaces that draw — and per its ruling, "if that player's hand is empty, they put the top card of their library into their graveyard. The player doesn't draw a card at all." So Addison's empty-hand draws are converted into mills, not draws.

Walking through the first resulting draw: Addison applies Alms Collector (Addison draws one, Noa draws one). Addison's single draw is then subject to Chains (empty hand) → it becomes a mill, no card drawn. Thought Reflection and Blood Scrivener never get to act, because the draw was replaced by a mill before resolving as a draw, and the Archive's doubling only applies to actual draws.

The same pattern repeats for each of the 10 draws while Addison's hand remains empty: each is funneled through Alms Collector and Chains and ends with Addison milling a card rather than gaining one, while Noa draws a card off Alms Collector.

## The bottom line

With best play, **Addison ends up with zero cards in their hand.** Each of the draws generated by the Lich is, on Addison's side, either redirected by Alms Collector (so the "draw two or more" never lands as multiple cards for Addison) or, where Addison draws a single card with an empty hand, replaced by Chains of Mephistopheles into a mill — "the player doesn't draw a card at all." The doublers (Thought Reflection, Blood Scrivener, and the Archive) never resolve into extra cards for Addison because Alms Collector intercepts every multi-draw instruction first and Chains stops every single empty-hand draw.

What else happens:
- **Noa draws up to 10 cards** — one per draw, via Alms Collector each time it intercepts a multi-card instruction.
- **Addison mills cards** off the top of their library via Chains for the single-card draws.
- **Addison gains no life and loses no life**: Words of Worship's life gain was converted by the Archive and then consumed by Nefarious Lich into draws, so Rain of Gore never triggers.

So the greatest number of cards Addison can have in hand at the end of this scenario is **zero**.

question: Andi controls Nightmare Shepherd and Terravore. Terravore then dies, which triggers Nightmare Shepherd. Andi decides to exile Terravore with the trigger. What is the toughness of the token?
expected_answer:
1/1. [707.9d] says that we don't copy the characteristic-defining abilities when applying a copy effect that sets toughness, which is intended to apply here.

(Nightmare Shepherd isn't generating a continuous effect, it's just defining the characteristics of the token it's creating, so as worded [707.9d] doesn't apply to it. That's not intentional though.)
model_answer:
Short answer: The token’s toughness is 1.

Step‑by‑step reasoning and citations:

1) Objects and characteristics: Terravore is a nontoken creature whose power and toughness are each defined by a printed ability (“Trample. Terravore's power and toughness are each equal to the number of land cards in all graveyards.”) — see the Terravore oracle text. The Terravore ruling states that the ability that defines its power and toughness “works in all zones” (Terravore ruling).

2) The death and trigger: Terravore dies, causing Nightmare Shepherd’s triggered ability (“Whenever another nontoken creature you control dies, you may exile it. If you do, create a token that's a copy of that creature, except it's 1/1 and it's a Nightmare…”) to trigger (Nightmare Shepherd oracle text).

3) Exiling the card: Andi chooses to exile Terravore as the triggered ability resolves. To exile an object is to put it into the exile zone from whatever zone it’s currently in (CR 406.2).

4) What the trigger creates: Nightmare Shepherd’s effect creates “a token that's a copy of that creature, except it's 1/1 …” (Nightmare Shepherd oracle text). The Shepherd rulings explicitly address copying creatures whose power/toughness are defined by an ability: “If the token is a copy of a creature whose power and toughness are defined by an ability (usually printed as */* or similar), the token doesn't copy the ability that defines its power and toughness. It remains a 1/1 creature.” (Nightmare Shepherd rulings).

5) Copying rules support: When making a copy, the copy acquires copiable values of the original, but Nightmare Shepherd’s replacement/creation text overrides the copy’s power/toughness by specifying it is a 1/1 (see CR 707.2 for how copying acquires copiable values and the Shepherd oracle text/ruling that the token is 1/1).

Conclusion: Because the Shepherd-created token does not copy Terravore’s P/T‑defining ability and is explicitly made a 1/1, the token’s toughness is 1 (Nightmare Shepherd oracle text and rulings; Terravore oracle and ruling; CR 406.2; CR 707.2).

# Determining the Token's Toughness

## The setup

Andi controls Nightmare Shepherd and Terravore. Terravore is a `*/*` creature whose oracle text states "Terravore's power and toughness are each equal to the number of land cards in all graveyards" — a characteristic-defining ability.

When Terravore dies, Nightmare Shepherd triggers: "you may exile it. If you do, create a token that's a copy of that creature, except it's 1/1 and it's a Nightmare in addition to its other types." Andi chooses to exile, so a copy token is created.

## What the token copies

The token copies the copiable values of Terravore (per CR 707.2, the copiable values are derived from the printed text — name, mana cost, types, rules text, power, toughness, etc.). So normally the token would carry Terravore's characteristic-defining ability that sets its power/toughness equal to land cards in graveyards.

However, Nightmare Shepherd's effect explicitly overrides this. The ruling for Nightmare Shepherd is directly on point:

> "If the token is a copy of a creature whose power and toughness are defined by an ability (usually printed as */* or similar), the token doesn't copy the ability that defines its power and toughness. It remains a 1/1 creature."

Terravore is exactly such a creature — its power and toughness are defined by an ability (printed as `*/*`). Therefore the token does **not** copy the power/toughness-defining ability, and Nightmare Shepherd's "except it's 1/1" clause applies.

## Answer

**The toughness of the token is 1.**

It is a 1/1 copy of Terravore (also a Nightmare). The characteristic-defining ability that would otherwise set its toughness equal to the number of land cards in all graveyards is not copied, so the "1/1" from Nightmare Shepherd's effect governs. By CR 302.4b, that means its toughness — the amount of damage needed to destroy it — is 1.

question: Azariah and Aubrey are playing a game of Two-Headed Giant. Azariah casts Warbriar Blessing targeting Aubrey's Glass Golem and has it fight itself. Does Glass Golem die?
expected_answer: This can't happen as described. Azariah cannot target Glass Golem with Warbriar Blessing as they must enchant a creature they control.
model_answer:
Short answer: No — Glass Golem can’t be made to fight itself with Warbriar Blessing. If, hypothetically, it somehow did fight itself and the fight actually happened, it would die.

Why it can’t fight itself: Warbriar Blessing reads “Enchant creature you control” and its triggered ability tells the enchanted creature to fight “up to one target creature you don’t control.” The enchanted creature is therefore a creature the aura’s controller controls, while the target must be a creature that controller does not control, so they cannot be the same object. If the ability’s target is illegal when the triggered ability tries to resolve, the ability doesn’t resolve (Warbriar Blessing ruling), and CR 701.14b says that if one or both creatures instructed to fight are illegal targets, neither fights or deals damage. So you cannot have the same Glass Golem be both the enchanted creature and the “target creature you don’t control” when the ability resolves.

What would happen if it did fight itself: If a creature actually fought itself, it deals damage to itself equal to twice its power (CR 701.14c). Warbriar Blessing also gives “Enchanted creature gets +0/+2,” so Glass Golem’s toughness would be 4 while its power is 6, and it would be dealt 12 damage (2 × 6). A creature with damage marked on it greater than or equal to its toughness is destroyed as a state‑based action (see CR 704.5g and CR 302.7). Therefore, if the fight resolved with Glass Golem fighting itself, it would be destroyed.

expected_answer: This can't happen as described. Azariah cannot target Glass Golem with Warbriar Blessing as they must enchant a creature they control.
model_answer:

Azariah can’t legally cast **Warbriar Blessing** targeting Aubrey’s **Glass Golem** as the Aura spell, because Warbriar Blessing has **“Enchant creature you control”**, and in Two-Headed Giant teammates’ resources/permanents are not shared; teammates can’t manipulate each other’s permanents, by **CR 810.5**. - With the starting Polyraptor only, the sequence of Forerunner resolutions while it’s alive is four times (F1, F2, F3, F4) because Forerunner dies when it has been dealt four points of damage (it had one +1/+1 counter). question: Avah controls a The Flame of Keld with 2 lore counters on it and Soul-Scar Mage. Avah puts the third lore counter on The Flame of Keld and resolves its final chapter ability. Avah then casts Wild Slash, targeting Nathanael's Sunastian Falconer. How many -1/-1 counters are placed on the Sunastian Falconer?
expected_answer: There are two replacement effects trying to apply to the damage being dealt, so Nathanael chooses which one to apply first. If they apply The Flame of Keld's effect first, Soul-Scar Mage's effect will then apply afterwards, and 4 -1/-1 counters will be placed on the Sunastian Falconer. If they apply the Soul-Scar Mage's effect first, the The Flame of Keld's effect will no longer be applicable and the Sunastian Falconer will only receive 2 -1/-1 counters.
model_answer:
Short answer: 2 −1/−1 counters (unless Nathanael chooses the other order, in which case it would be 4).

Explanation

- The Flame of Keld’s III chapter ability is in effect and reads “If a red source you control would deal damage to a permanent or player this turn, it deals that much damage plus 2 to that permanent or player instead.” (Saga resolved; see CR 714.2e.)
- Wild Slash would deal 2 damage to Sunastian Falconer. Soul-Scar Mage’s replacement effect says “If a source you control would deal noncombat damage to a creature an opponent controls, put that many −1/−1 counters on that creature instead.”
- Damage is dealt as modified by replacement and prevention effects (see CR 120.4b). Two replacement effects are attempting to modify the same damage event: The Flame of Keld’s “+2” effect and Soul-Scar Mage’s “replace damage with −1/−1 counters.”
- When two or more replacement and/or prevention effects are trying to modify an event, the affected object’s controller (or the affected player) chooses which to apply (CR 616.1). In this case the affected object is the Falconer, so its controller, Nathanael, chooses the order in which to apply those effects (this is also what the card rulings for Soul-Scar Mage/The Flame explain in examples).
- If Nathanael applies Soul-Scar Mage’s replacement first, the 2 damage from Wild Slash is replaced by placing two −1/−1 counters on the Falconer; after that there is no damage event left for The Flame’s “+2” effect to modify, so the result is 2 −1/−1 counters.
- If Nathanael instead applies The Flame’s effect first, Wild Slash’s damage becomes 4, and then Soul-Scar Mage would replace that 4 damage with four −1/−1 counters.

Therefore, with a rational choice by Nathanael to minimize counters, the Falconer gets 2 −1/−1 counters.

# Ordering the Replacement Effects

By CR 120.4b, damage is dealt as modified by replacement effects. Both The Flame of Keld's effect and Soul-Scar Mage's effect would apply to the same damage event. When multiple replacement effects apply to the same damage, the controller of the affected creature chooses the order — here that's **Nathanael** (the Falconer's controller). question: Avery controls a Spellbinder that exiled Plummet. Nasir controls a Nevermore that named "Plummet". As Spellbinder's second ability resolves, what happens?
expected_answer: Avery copies the Plummet in exile. Avery can't cast the copy, so it remains in exile. It ceases to exist as soon as Spellbinder's ability finishes resolving.
model_answer:
Short answer: You cannot cast the copy. When Spellbinder's ability resolves you may create the copy, but Nevermore prevents you from beginning to cast it, so no Plummet spell is put on the stack and no creature is destroyed.

Step-by-step reasoning with cited sources

1) What Spellbinder's ability does on resolution
- Spellbinder’s triggered/resolving text and its ruling say the ability “allows you to create a copy of the imprinted instant card in the Exile zone … and then cast it without paying its mana cost,” and that “The creation of the copy and then the casting of the copy are both optional.” (Spellbinder oracle and rulings). So the resolution proceeds by (a) optionally creating a copy and (b) if you do, optionally attempting to cast that copy.

2) Casting the copy during the ability’s resolution
- When an effect specifically allows a player to cast a spell during resolution, that casting is done by following rules 601.2a–i (with the special exception about priority) (CR 608.2g). CR 601.2 states “A player must be legally allowed to cast the spell to begin this process” (CR 601.2).

3) Nevermore’s restriction
- Nevermore’s oracle text/ruling: “Spells with the chosen name can’t be cast.” Nasir named “Plummet,” so spells named Plummet can’t be cast while that Nevermore is in effect (Nevermore oracle/rulings).

4) Apply the restriction to the copy
- The copy Spellbinder creates is a copy of Plummet, so it would have the name Plummet and therefore is a “spell with the chosen name” when you attempt to cast it. Because Nevermore says such spells can’t be cast, you are not legally allowed to begin the casting process required by CR 601.2; therefore you cannot cast the copy (Nevermore ruling + CR 601.2 + CR 608.2g; Spellbinder ruling explaining creation then casting).

5) Result
- You may choose whether to create the copy at all (Spellbinder ruling). If you choose not to create it, nothing happens. If you choose to create the copy, you then attempt to cast it but cannot because Nevermore prohibits casting it, so no spell is put onto the stack and the ability finishes resolving (CR 608.2g and CR 608.2n say the ability finishes resolving). In either case, no Plummet resolves and no creature is destroyed.

Explicit commitment: No — the copy cannot be cast while Nevermore names “Plummet.” The end result is that no Plummet spell is put on the stack and the target creature with flying is not destroyed. If you want the exact line-by-line citations again, they are: Spellbinder ruling (copy then cast; both optional), Nevermore oracle/ruling (“Spells with the chosen name can’t be cast”), CR 608.2g (casting during resolution follows 601.2a–i), CR 601.2 (you must be legally allowed to cast a spell), and CR 608.2n (ability finishes resolving).  

If you want a ruling about whether the copy object remains in exile after the ability finishes, the provided context does not state the precise zone persistence or disappearance behavior for a created-but-uncast copy, so I cannot confidently say more than that the copy was not cast and the ability finished resolving.

question: It is currently Alijah's cleanup step, and Nathalie cast Worst Fears last turn. Alijah discards Worldspine Wurm and Emrakul, the Aeons Torn to hand size. Which player chooses in what order to put the triggers onto the stack?
expected_answer: Nathalie does. Only effects worded as "this turn" or "until end of turn" wear off in the cleanup step. Nathalie controls Alijah during the entire turn.
model_answer:
Short answer: Alijah chooses the order. Yes.

Reasoning, step by step with citations to the retrieved context:

1) The discards happen as the cleanup-step hand-size action. By CR 514.1 the active player first discards to their maximum hand size. (CR 514.1) Worldspine Wurm’s and Emrakul’s oracle texts each read “When [card] is put into a graveyard from anywhere …”, so both abilities trigger when those cards are put into Alijah’s graveyard.

2) Those triggered abilities are handled during the cleanup-step trigger placement. CR 514.3a says after the discard the game checks for triggered abilities waiting to be put onto the stack (and then they are put on the stack), and CR 117.5 explains that triggered abilities are put on the stack each time a player would get priority. (CR 514.3a; CR 117.5)

3) Who puts triggered abilities onto the stack and in what order is governed by the triggered-ability placement rules. CR 603.3 says once an ability has triggered, its controller puts it on the stack the next time a player would receive priority. CR 603.3b explains the two-part APNAP process: each player, in APNAP order (see CR 101.4), first puts triggered abilities they control with trigger conditions that aren’t other abilities triggering on the stack in any order they choose, then in APNAP order they put remaining triggered abilities they control. CR 101.4 defines APNAP order (active player, then nonactive players in turn order). (CR 603.3; CR 603.3b; CR 101.4)

4) Worst Fears does not make Nathalie the controller of Alijah’s permanents/spells/abilities; it only makes Nathalie control the player (the decisions that player would make). The Worst Fears ruling explicitly says “You only control the player. You don't control any of their permanents, spells, or abilities.” Therefore Nathalie does not become the controller of the triggered abilities on Alijah’s cards; those triggers are still controlled by Alijah. (Worst Fears ruling: “You only control the player. You don't control any of their permanents, spells, or abilities.”)

5) Putting those triggered abilities onto the stack and choosing their relative order is done by the abilities’ controller(s) in APNAP order (CR 603.3b & CR 101.4). Because it is Alijah’s turn (Alijah is the active player), Alijah is the first player in APNAP order and therefore Alijah (the controller of the two triggers) chooses the order to put Worldspine Wurm’s trigger and Emrakul’s trigger onto the stack. (CR 603.3b; CR 101.4)

6) Note the distinction: while Nathalie (who cast Worst Fears) will make any choices that Alijah would be required to make by those triggered abilities during this turn (because Worst Fears makes Nathalie make choices for Alijah), that does not change who controls the triggered abilities or who decides the order they are placed on the stack. The Worst Fears ruling says you “make all choices and decisions that player is allowed to make … including … decisions called for by triggered abilities,” but it also separately states you don’t control the player’s permanents/spells/abilities. Thus ordering the placement is done by the abilities’ controller (Alijah), while any choices the abilities themselves request during resolution will be made by Nathalie as the controller of the player for that turn. (Worst Fears rulings; CR 603.3b)

Conclusion: Alijah chooses the order in which the Emrakul and Worldspine Wurm “put into a graveyard” triggers are placed on the stack (Alijah is the controller of those triggered abilities and is the active player, so by CR 603.3b and CR 101.4 they place them first and choose their order).

Per the Worst Fears rulings, while controlling another player, Nathalie "make[s] all choices and decisions that player is allowed to make or is told to make during that turn," including "any decisions called for by triggered abilities or for any other reason."

# Step 2: Identify the triggers

In the cleanup step, Alijah discards down to maximum hand size (CR 514.1 — this turn-based action doesn't use the stack). Alijah discards two cards:

- **Worldspine Wurm** — "When Worldspine Wurm is put into a graveyard from anywhere, shuffle it into its owner's library." This is a triggered ability (per its ruling).
- **Emrakul, the Aeons Torn** — "When Emrakul is put into a graveyard from anywhere, its owner shuffles their graveyard into their library." Also a triggered ability.

Both triggers are controlled by Alijah (they are abilities of cards owned/discarded by Alijah, and the controlling player of the triggered ability is the player who controlled the source — Alijah).

# Step 3: Triggers being put on the stack

Under CR 514.3a, during the cleanup step the game checks for triggered abilities waiting to be put on the stack; if any exist they are put on the stack and the active player gets priority. The order in which one player's own simultaneous triggers go on the stack is governed by CR 603.3b: each player, in APNAP order, "puts each triggered ability they control … on the stack in any order they choose," referencing CR 101.4.

Both triggers are controlled by Alijah, so it is Alijah's controller who chooses the order among them.

# Step 4: Who actually makes the choice

Here is the key point. The triggers are controlled by Alijah, and ordering them is a decision called for by the game rules (CR 603.3b / 101.4). But Nathalie is currently controlling Alijah via Worst Fears. The Worst Fears rulings state that while controlling another player, Nathalie makes "all decisions called for by triggered abilities or for any other reason" that Alijah would make.

Ordering Alijah's own triggers on the stack is exactly such a decision. Therefore **Nathalie** — as the player controlling Alijah this turn — makes the choice of what order to put Alijah's two triggers on the stack.

# Answer

**Nathalie chooses the order.** Although both the Worldspine Wurm and Emrakul triggers are controlled by Alijah and ordered by "their controller" under CR 603.3b (in APNAP order per CR 101.4), Nathalie is controlling Alijah this turn due to Worst Fears. Per the Worst Fears rulings, Nathalie makes all of Alijah's decisions called for by the game rules and by triggered abilities — and that includes deciding the order in which these two triggers are placed on the stack during the cleanup step (CR 514.3a).

question: It is currently Alijah's cleanup step, and Nathalie cast Worst Fears last turn. Alijah discards Worldspine Wurm and Emrakul, the Aeons Torn to hand size. Which player chooses in what order to put the triggers onto the stack?
expected_answer: Nathalie does. Only effects worded as "this turn" or "until end of turn" wear off in the cleanup step. Nathalie controls Alijah during the entire turn.

Looking at this step-by-step:

## The Situation

During Alijah's cleanup step, Alijah (the active player) discards Worldspine Wurm and Emrakul, the Aeons Torn to hand size. Nathalie cast Worst Fears last turn, so Nathalie controls Alijah during this turn.

## The Triggered Abilities

When both cards are put into the graveyard from discarding:
- **Worldspine Wurm** triggers: "When Worldspine Wurm is put into a graveyard from anywhere, shuffle it into its owner's library."
- **Emrakul, the Aeons Torn** triggers: "When Emrakul is put into a graveyard from anywhere, its owner shuffles their graveyard into their library."

Both abilities are controlled by Alijah (their owner).

## Who Orders the Triggers?

By **CR 603.3**, when an ability triggers, "its controller puts it on the stack." The controller of a triggered ability is the player who controls its source at the time it triggered.

By **CR 603.3b**, "If multiple abilities have triggered since the last time a player received priority, the abilities are placed on the stack in a two-part process. First, each player, in APNAP order, puts each triggered ability they control... on the stack in any order they choose."

The key question is: who is the controller of these triggered abilities?

According to Worst Fears rulings: "You only control the player. You don't control any of their permanents, spells, or abilities." and "While controlling another player, you make all choices and decisions that player is allowed to make or is told to make during that turn."

However, the triggered abilities are controlled by Alijah (as the owner of the cards). The abilities themselves are not controlled by Nathalie—she controls the *player* Alijah, not Alijah's abilities.

But Worst Fears states: "you make all choices and decisions that player is allowed to make or is told to make during that turn." By **CR 603.3b**, Alijah is told to choose the order to put their triggered abilities on the stack.

## Answer

**Nathalie chooses the order.** 

While Alijah is the controller of both triggered abilities (as owner of the cards), Nathalie controls Alijah as a player due to Worst Fears. By CR 603.3b, when multiple triggered abilities controlled by the same player need to be put on the stack, that player chooses the order. Since Worst Fears allows Nathalie to "make all choices and decisions that player is allowed to make," Nathalie makes this ordering decision on Alijah's behalf.

question: Axl controls Silhana Ledgewalker and an Ixalan's Binding that exiled Nadia's Bewitching Leechcraft. Nadia casts Spring Cleaning targeting Ixalan's Binding. What happens as the Bewitching Leechcraft returns to the battlefield?
expected_answer: It enchants Silhana Ledgewalker. Auras entering the battlefield from a zone other than the stack must enchant a legal object. It doesn't target anything, so hexproof doesn't matter.
model_answer:
Spring Cleaning destroys Ixalan’s Binding. Ixalan’s Binding’s official ruling states that the exiled card returns to the battlefield immediately after Ixalan’s Binding leaves the battlefield and that nothing happens between those two events. That is also consistent with how one‑shot effects that move objects “until” an event work (see CR 610.3); the returned object comes back under its owner’s control (CR 610.3c). So Bewitching Leechcraft returns to the battlefield immediately under Nadia’s control.

Because Bewitching Leechcraft is entering the battlefield by an effect (not by resolving as an Aura spell), CR 303.4f applies: as it enters the battlefield its controller (Nadia) chooses what it will enchant, and she must choose a legal object or player for the Aura. Choosing an object in this way is not the same as “targeting” (CR 115.10a), so Silhana Ledgewalker’s hexproof (CR 702.11) does not prevent the Aura from entering attached to it. Assuming Silhana is still a legal creature to be enchanted, Nadia may choose to have Bewitching Leechcraft enter attached to Silhana.

When the Aura enters attached to Silhana, Bewitching Leechcraft’s “When this Aura enters, tap enchanted creature.” ability will trigger (triggered abilities can trigger when objects enter the battlefield; see CR 117.2a) and, when that triggered ability resolves, it will tap Silhana. The Aura’s continuous effect that changes the enchanted creature’s untap step will also apply while it remains attached.

If there were no legal creature for the Aura to enchant as it entered, the Aura wouldn’t successfully enter the battlefield (or, if it were entering from the stack, it would be put into its owner’s graveyard) per CR 303.4g and CR 704.5m.
